tickets.title: Library → Component → PMA3-83LNW+ → Component Layout
tickets.prompt:
GENERATE FOOTPRINT — PMA3-83LNW+
=======================================================

Read the datasheet to find the physical package dimensions, then build and save the footprint.

STEPS:
1. Read raw datasheet text:  library/PMA3_83LNW/raw_text.txt
   - Find the package/mechanical section (look for "Package", "Dimensions", "Outline", "Land Pattern")
   - Note: package type is "12-lead MCLP 3x3x0.89mm (DQ1225)", pin count is 13

2. Determine the footprint name:
   - Use the standard industry name if it matches a known package (e.g. "QFN-16-3x3", "SOIC-8", "DIP-14")
   - If no standard name applies, use the part number: "PMA3-83LNW+"

3. Build the footprint JSON (all dimensions in mm):
{
  "name": "<footprint-name>",
  "description": "PMA3-83LNW+ — 12-lead MCLP 3x3x0.89mm (DQ1225)",
  "pads": [
    { "number": "1", "name": "PIN1", "x": 0, "y": 0, "type": "smd", "shape": "rect", "size_x": 0.6, "size_y": 1.5 }
  ],
  "courtyard": { "x": -2, "y": -2, "w": 4, "h": 4 }
}

PIN NAMES for this component:
  2: RF-IN
  8: RF-OUT/DC-IN
  0: GND (Paddle)
  1: NC
  3: NC
  4: NC
  5: NC
  6: NC
  7: NC
  9: NC
  10: NC
  11: NC (float!)
  12: NC (float!)

4. Save the footprint:
   PUT http://localhost:3030/api/footprints/<footprint-name>
   Body: the JSON above

5. Assign to profile:
   PUT http://localhost:3030/api/library/PMA3_83LNW/footprint
   Body: { "footprint": "<footprint-name>" }

IMPORTANT:
- All pad x/y coordinates are relative to the component center (0,0)
- "type" is "smd" for surface-mount or "thru_hole" for through-hole pads
- "shape" is "rect" or "circle"
- Courtyard x/y is the top-left corner offset from center, w/h are total dimensions
- Match pin numbers exactly to profile pins above

WHEN DONE — mark this ticket complete:
PUT http://localhost:3030/api/gen-tickets/4
Body: { "status": "done" }

tickets.title: Library → Component → QPA9510 → Example Schematic
tickets.prompt:
REBUILD EXAMPLE CIRCUIT — QPA9510
=======================================================

Build a correct application circuit schematic for QPA9510 and write it to:
library/QPA9510_DATA_SHEET/profile.json  →  field: "example_circuit"

Use PUT /api/library/QPA9510_DATA_SHEET/example_circuit with the circuit JSON body.

CIRCUIT FORMAT:
{
  "components": [
    { "id": "u1", "slug": "QPA9510_DATA_SHEET", "symType": "amplifier", "designator": "U1", "value": "QPA9510", "x": 320, "y": 240, "rotation": 0 },
    { "id": "c1", "slug": "CAPACITOR", "symType": "capacitor", "designator": "C1", "value": "100nF", "x": 200, "y": 240, "rotation": 0 }
  ],
  "wires": [
    { "id": "w1", "points": [{"x": 280, "y": 240}, {"x": 220, "y": 240}] }
  ],
  "labels": [
    { "id": "lbl1", "name": "VCC", "x": 320, "y": 120, "rotation": 0 }
  ]
}

SLUG NAMES for passives: "CAPACITOR", "INDUCTOR", "RESISTOR", "VCC", "GND"
IC slug for this component: "QPA9510_DATA_SHEET"

COORDINATE RULES:
- Grid: 20 units. All x/y must be multiples of 20.
- Typical range: x 80–600, y 60–500
- Wire points use L-routing: add an elbow point where needed
- VCC symbols point up (rotation:0), GND point down (rotation:0)
- Capacitor/inductor ports: rotation:0 → ports at (0,-20) top and (0,+20) bottom; rotation:1 → ports at (-20,0) left and (+20,0) right

APPLICATION CIRCUIT TO IMPLEMENT:
- 900MHz GSM (QPA9510EVB-01): L1=11nH interstage shunt on VCC1, L3=8.8nH output match, L4=3.6nH, L6=1.6nH, L2/L7=120Ω ferrite beads on supply, R1=180Ω APC bias, C11=5.6pF input, C14/C15=47pF output
- 850MHz: L1=11nH, L3=8.8nH, L4=2.2nH, L6=1.6nH, R1=220Ω. At 850MHz: 35dB gain, +34 dBm P1dB, 57% efficiency
- 450MHz (QPA9510EVB-02): L1=12nH, L3=1µH, L4=2.7nH, L6=6.8nH. At 450MHz: 42.4 dB gain, +33.2 dBm P1dB, 49.5% efficiency
- 169MHz (QPA9510EVB-03): L1=12nH, L3=1µH, L4=2.7nH, L6=6.8nH. At 169MHz: 41.8 dB gain, +29.4 dBm P1dB, 48.8% efficiency

REQUIRED PASSIVES (must all be present):
- capacitor DC-blocking cap (e.g. 56pF for 900MHz, see EVB BOM): Series on RF_IN (pad 3) — DC blocking capacitor explicitly required on RF IN — datasheet pad description p.23
- inductor Shunt inductance (e.g. 11nH for 900MHz): VCC1 (pad 1) to GND — Required for interstage matching — pin 1 forms the shunt inductance, datasheet p.23
- inductor Shunt inductance (frequency dependent): VCC2 (pads 5,6) to GND — Required for interstage matching — datasheet p.23
- capacitor 1000pF: VCC supply to GND (close to device) — Supply bypass — per all evaluation board BOMs p.7/11/18
- capacitor 10nF (10000pF): VCC supply to GND (close to device) — Low-frequency supply bypass — per all evaluation board BOMs
- capacitor 3.3µF tantalum: VCC supply to GND (bulk) — Bulk supply bypass — per all EVB BOMs
- capacitor Bypass cap (per APC application): APC1 (pad 16) to GND — Shunt bypass capacitor explicitly required on APC1 — datasheet pad description p.23
- capacitor Series resonator cap (frequency dependent): 2F0 (pad 8) to GND — Forms second harmonic trap with bonding wire to improve efficiency and reduce spurious — datasheet p.23
- network Output matching network (L/C, frequency dependent — see EVB BOMs): RF OUT (pads 9–12) to 50Ω load — External matching network required for optimum performance — datasheet p.23

KEY PINS (first 17 of 17):
- Pin 1 VCC1: power
- Pin 2 GND1: gnd
- Pin 3 RF_IN: input
- Pin 4 GND2: gnd
- Pin 5 VCC2: power
- Pin 6 VCC2: power
- Pin 7 NC: passive
- Pin 8 2F0: passive
- Pin 9 RF_OUT: output
- Pin 10 RF_OUT: output
- Pin 11 RF_OUT: output
- Pin 12 RF_OUT: output
- Pin 13 NC: passive
- Pin 14 VCC: power
- Pin 15 APC2: input
- Pin 16 APC1: input
- Pin 17 GND_PADDLE: gnd

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Read library/QPA9510_DATA_SHEET/profile.json to get the full pin list and application circuit description
2. Build a wired schematic with ALL required passives connected correctly
3. Verify wire endpoints match actual port positions (computed from component center + port dx/dy after rotation)
4. Use net labels (VCC, GND, RF_IN, RF_OUT, etc.) instead of wires for power rails
5. PUT the circuit JSON to /api/library/QPA9510_DATA_SHEET/example_circuit
6. Update the ticket status to done with observations describing what you built

WHEN DONE — mark this ticket complete:
PUT http://localhost:3030/api/gen-tickets/6
Body: { "status": "done" }

tickets.title: Rebuild profile: QPA9510
tickets.prompt:
REBUILD COMPONENT PROFILE — QPA9510
=======================================================

You are verifying and correcting the component profile for QPA9510.
Write the corrected profile to: library/QPA9510_DATA_SHEET/profile.json

VERIFICATION TASKS (do all of these):
1. Read the datasheet text below carefully
2. Check every pin: does pin NUMBER match the NAME given in the datasheet?
   - If a pin is misnamed → correct it
   - If a pin number is wrong → fix it
   - If a pin exists in the datasheet but is missing from the profile → add it
   - If a pin is in the profile but NOT in the datasheet → remove it
3. Recount the total pin count — the final profile must match the datasheet exactly
4. Set "designator" field to "U" (schematic reference designator prefix)
5. Preserve ALL human_corrections — never delete them
6. Keep "builtin": false unchanged
7. Set "status": "parsed" and "confidence": "HIGH" if all pins are verified

CURRENT DESIGNATOR: (not set — must be added as "designator": "U")

USER NOTES: GaAs PHEMT technology. Thermal resistance Θjc = 12.5°C/W — backside paddle soldering and PCB vias mandatory for thermal management at high power. The 2F0 pin improves harmonic suppression and efficiency — do not leave floating at operating frequencies. Output matching network is highly frequency-dependent; use the relevant EVB BOM as the starting point and tune from there. Quiescent current: 208–230mA. Standby current: 58µA at room temp / 10µA at 85°C.

CURRENT PROFILE (17 pins):

  "part_number": "QPA9510",
  "description": "3V GaAs PHEMT high-power amplifier, 100–1000 MHz, 34 dB gain, +35 dBm P1dB, 55% efficiency, analog power control",

DATASHEET TEXT:


    
Data Sheet, February 10, 2026 
Subject to change without notice | All rights reserved 
1 of 27 www.qorvo.com   
 
  
 
 
QPA9510 
3V General Purpose/GSM Power Amplifier 
®
 
16 Pad 3 x 3 mm QFN Package 
Key Features 
 
• 100 – 1000 MHz Operating Frequency Range 
• +2.8 V to +4.8 V Single Supply  
• 34 dB Max Gain with Analog Gain Control  
• 55% Efficiency 
• Achieves +35 dBm P1dB 
• Supports GSM and E-GSM 
• 3 x 3 mm QFN Package 
Product Overview 
 
The QPA9510 is  a  high-power,  high-gain,  high-efficiency 
power amplifier. The device is manufactured with Qorvo’s 
advanced GaAs process. The amplifier  provides 34 dB of 
max gain and able to achieve +35 dBm of P1dB along with 
flexibility in bias settings. 
 
QPA9510 is  designed  for  use  as  the  final  RF  amplifier  in 
GSM  hand-held  equipment  in  900  MHz  band and  other 
applications in the UHF bands. An analog on-board power 
controller  provides  over  70  dB  range  of gain adjustment. 
This control also allows for power down with a voltage equal 
to the logic “Low” to set the device in standby mode.  
 
The QPA9510 is  tunable  over  any  sub-bands  in  the 
operating range to optimize performance. The amplifiers sit 
in a 3mm x 3mm compact QFN package. 
Applications 
 
• FM Radios 
• Public Safety Radios 
• UHF Applications 
• Meter Readers 
• Telematics 
• RFID 
• ISM Systems 
• 3V GSM Cellular Handsets

    C6 8.2pF: from Pin 8 (220,340) down to GND net label

Right side (place components to the RIGHT of IC, x=440 to 700):
  APC control (y~200-260):

    C5 100nF: from Pin 16 APC1 (420,220) to GND net label
  VCC supply decoupling (y~260):
    C12 100pF + C13 10nF: from Pin 14 VCC (420,260) to GND
  RF OUTPUT matching chain (pins 9-12, connect all to single RF_OUT bus then chain):
    RF_OUT bus wire from pins 9(220,360), 10(420,340), 11(420,320), 12(420,300)
    L3 8.8nH series → L4 2.2nH series → L6 1.6nH series
    C7 47pF shunt between L3-L4, C8 47pF shunt after L6, all to GND
    RF_OUT net label at far right

STEP 5 — CIRCUIT JSON RULES
- Grid: 20 units. ALL x/y must be multiples of 20.
- Range: x 20–740, y 60–540 (larger canvas to fit all components)
- slugs: CAPACITOR, INDUCTOR, RESISTOR, VCC, GND, IC slug: QPA9510_DATA_SHEET
- rotation 0 = vertical (ports top/bottom at ±20), rotation 1 = horizontal (ports left/right at ±20)
- Use net LABELS (not GND/VCC symbols) for power: {"id":"lbl_x","name":"GND","x":...,"y":...,"rotation":0}
- Every pin that needs connecting MUST have a wire or label
- All IDs must be unique
- After building: count components. Should be 20+ passives plus U1.

PMA3-83LNW+
PRODUCT OVERVIEW
The PMA3-83LNW+ is a PHEMT based wideband, low noise MMIC amplifier with a unique combination of low noise, high IP3, 
and flat gain over wideband making it ideal for sensitive, high-dynamic-range receiver applications.  This design operates on a 
single 5V or 6V supply, is well matched for 50Ω and comes in a tiny, low profile package (3 x 3 x 0.89mm), accommodating dense 
circuit board layouts.
FeatureAdvantages
Low noise, 1.2 dB at 2 GHzEnables lower system noise figure performance.
High IP3
 y+37 dBm at 2 GHz
 y+29 dBm at 8 GHz
Combination of low noise and high IP3 makes this MMIC amplifier ideal for use in low noise receiver front end 
(RFE) as it gives the user advantages of sensitivity and two-tone IM performance at both ends of the dynamic 
range.
Low operating voltage, 5V/6V.Achieves high IP3 using low voltage.
3 x 3mm 12-lead MCLP package
Tiny footprint saves space in dense layouts while providing low inductance, repeatable transitions, and excellent 
thermal contact to the PCB.
Wide bandwidth with flat gain
 y±0.6 dB over 0.4 to 7 GHz
 y±1.5 dB over 0.4 to 8 GHz
Enables a single amplifier to be used in many wideband applications including defense, instrumentation and more.

AP2112 
Document number: DS39724  Rev. 2 - 2 
1 of 18 
www.diodes.com  
June 2017 
© Diodes Incorporated 
 
AP2112 
 
600mA CMOS LDO REGULATOR WITH ENABLE 
 
Description 
The  AP2112  is  CMOS  process  low  dropout  linear  regulator  with 
enable  function,  the  regulator  delivers  a  guaranteed  600mA  (min.) 
continuous load current.  
 
The  AP2112 is  available  with  a  fixed output  voltage  of 1.2V,  1.8V, 
2.5V, 2.6V, or 3.3V. The LDO has an output accuracy of ±1.5% and a 
very  fast  loop  response  providing excellent performance for  dealing 
with line and load transients. The AP2112 includes an auto discharge 
function which  connects  the  output  to  ground  via  60 of  resistance 
when the device is disabled. 
 
The regulator features low power consumption, and provides SOT25, 
SOT89-5,  and  SO-8  packages. Previously  SOT-23-5,  SOT-89-5  and 
SOIC-8 packages were respectively identified as SOT23-5, SOT89-5 
and  SO-8 but  have been  renamed  to  match  the  latest  Diodes 
Incorporated‘s nomenclature. 
 
Features 
 Output Voltage Accuracy: ±1.5% 
 Output Current: 600mA (Min.) 
 Foldback Short Current Protection: 50mA 
 Enable Function to Turn ON/OFF V
OUT

 Low Quiescent Current: 55μA (Typ.) 
 Low Standby Current: 0.01μA (Typ.) 
 Low Output Noise: 50μV

Aluminum Electrolytic 
 Operation Temperature Range: -40°C to +85°C 
 ESD: MM 400V, HBM 4000V  
 Totally Lead-Free & Fully RoHS Compliant (Notes 1 & 2)

Applications

Circuit JSON format:
{
  "components": [
    { "id": "u1", "slug": "AP2112", "symType": "ic", "designator": "U1", "value": "AP2112", "x": 320, "y": 240, "rotation": 0 },
    { "id": "c1", "slug": "CAPACITOR", "symType": "capacitor", "designator": "C1", "value": "100nF", "x": 200, "y": 240, "rotation": 0 }
  ],
  "wires": [
    { "id": "w1", "points": [{"x": 280, "y": 240}, {"x": 220, "y": 240}] }
  ],
  "labels": [
    { "id": "lbl1", "name": "VCC", "x": 320, "y": 120, "rotation": 0 }
  ]
}

SLUG NAMES: "CAPACITOR", "INDUCTOR", "RESISTOR", "VCC", "GND" — IC slug: "AP2112"

COORDINATE RULES:
- Grid: 20 units. All x/y must be multiples of 20.
- Range: x 80–600, y 60–500
- Wires use L-routing (add elbow point)
- VCC rotation:0 (up), GND rotation:0 (down)
- Passive ports: rotation:0 → top (0,-20) / bottom (0,+20); rotation:1 → left (-20,0) / right (+20,0)

APPLICATION CIRCUITS FROM PROFILE:
- Typical Application Circuit (p.2): Power path: VIN source → CIN (1μF ceramic, VIN to GND) → VIN pin (SOT25 pin 1) → [AP2112] → VOUT pin (SOT25 pin 5) → COUT (1μF ceramic, VOUT to GND) → load. Enable path: VEN logic signal → EN pin (SOT25 pin 3); tie EN to VIN for always-on operation. When EN is HIGH regulator is ON; when EN is LOW regulator is OFF and VOUT discharges to GND via internal 60Ω resistor.

REQUIRED PASSIVES (minimum set from profile — EVB BOM may have MORE):
- capacitor 1.0μF ceramic: VIN to GND, as close to VIN pin as possible — CIN — input bypass capacitor. Required for stability and transient response. Recommended X7R or X5R dielectric.
- capacitor 1.0μF ceramic: VOUT to GND, as close to VOUT pin as possible — COUT — output bypass capacitor. Required for stability. Stable with ceramic, tantalum, and aluminum electrolytic. Recommended X7R or X5R dielectric if ceramic.

ALL PINS (5 total):
  Pin 1 VIN [power]
  Pin 2 GND [gnd]
  Pin 3 EN [input]
  Pin 4 NC [passive]
  Pin 5 VOUT [output]

RULES:
- Every pin used in the datasheet app circuit must be connected
- Include ALL components from the EVB BOM / Typical Application — do not skip any
- Use net labels for power/ground instead of running wires across the schematic
- Wire endpoints must hit exact port positions (center + port offset after rotation)
- All IDs must be unique strings
- After building, count your components vs the BOM — they should match

ADDITIONAL INSTRUCTIONS FROM USER:
Capacitors are unconnected and resistors are missing. I need this to output 3.3v

tickets.prompt:
GENERATE FOOTPRINT — AP2112
=======================================================

Read the datasheet to find the physical package dimensions, then build and save the footprint.

STEPS:
1. Read raw datasheet text:  library/AP2112/raw_text.txt
   - Find the package/mechanical section (look for "Package", "Dimensions", "Outline", "Land Pattern")
   - Note: package type is "SOT25 (5-lead), SOT89-5 Option 1 (R5 Package), SOT89-5 Option 2 (R5A Package), SO-8 (8-lead)", pin count is 5

2. Determine the footprint name:
   - Use the standard industry name if it matches a known package (e.g. "QFN-16-3x3", "SOIC-8", "DIP-14")
   - If no standard name applies, use the part number: "AP2112"

3. Build the footprint JSON (all dimensions in mm):
{
  "name": "<footprint-name>",
  "description": "AP2112 — SOT25 (5-lead), SOT89-5 Option 1 (R5 Package), SOT89-5 Option 2 (R5A Package), SO-8 (8-lead)",
  "pads": [
    { "number": "1", "name": "PIN1", "x": 0, "y": 0, "type": "smd", "shape": "rect", "size_x": 0.6, "size_y": 1.5 }
  ],
  "courtyard": { "x": -2, "y": -2, "w": 4, "h": 4 }
}

PIN NAMES for this component:
  1: VIN
  2: GND
  3: EN
  4: NC
  5: VOUT

4. Save the footprint:
   PUT http://localhost:3030/api/footprints/<footprint-name>
   Body: the JSON above

5. Assign to profile:
   PUT http://localhost:3030/api/library/AP2112/footprint
   Body: { "footprint": "<footprint-name>" }

IMPORTANT:
- All pad x/y coordinates are relative to the component center (0,0)
- "type" is "smd" for surface-mount or "thru_hole" for through-hole pads
- "shape" is "rect" or "circle"
- Courtyard x/y is the top-left corner offset from center, w/h are total dimensions
- Match pin numbers exactly to profile pins above

STEPS:
1. Read raw datasheet text:  library/AP2112/raw_text.txt
   - Find the package/mechanical section (look for "Package", "Dimensions", "Outline", "Land Pattern")
   - Note: package type is "SOT25 (5-lead), SOT89-5 Option 1 (R5 Package), SOT89-5 Option 2 (R5A Package), SO-8 (8-lead)", pin count is 5

2. Determine the footprint name:
   - Use the standard industry name if it matches a known package (e.g. "QFN-16-3x3", "SOIC-8", "DIP-14")
   - If no standard name applies, use the part number: "AP2112"

3. Build the footprint JSON (all dimensions in mm):
{
  "name": "<footprint-name>",
  "description": "AP2112 — SOT25 (5-lead), SOT89-5 Option 1 (R5 Package), SOT89-5 Option 2 (R5A Package), SO-8 (8-lead)",
  "pads": [
    { "number": "1", "name": "PIN1", "x": 0, "y": 0, "type": "smd", "shape": "rect", "size_x": 0.6, "size_y": 1.5 }
  ],
  "courtyard": { "x": -2, "y": -2, "w": 4, "h": 4 }
}

PIN NAMES for this component:
  1: VIN
  2: GND
  3: EN
  4: NC
  5: VOUT

4. Save the footprint:
   PUT http://localhost:3030/api/footprints/<footprint-name>
   Body: the JSON above

5. Assign to profile:
   PUT http://localhost:3030/api/library/AP2112/footprint
   Body: { "footprint": "<footprint-name>" }

IMPORTANT:
- All pad x/y coordinates are relative to the component center (0,0)
- "type" is "smd" for surface-mount or "thru_hole" for through-hole pads
- "shape" is "rect" or "circle"
- Courtyard x/y is the top-left corner offset from center, w/h are total dimensions
- Match pin numbers exactly to profile pins above

WHEN DONE — mark this ticket complete:
PUT http://localhost:3030/api/gen-tickets/13
Body: { "status": "done" }

AP2112 
Document number: DS39724  Rev. 2 - 2 
1 of 18 
www.diodes.com  
June 2017 
© Diodes Incorporated 
 
AP2112 
 
600mA CMOS LDO REGULATOR WITH ENABLE 
 
Description 
The  AP2112  is  CMOS  process  low  dropout  linear  regulator  with 
enable  function,  the  regulator  delivers  a  guaranteed  600mA  (min.) 
continuous load current.  
 
The  AP2112 is  available  with  a  fixed output  voltage  of 1.2V,  1.8V, 
2.5V, 2.6V, or 3.3V. The LDO has an output accuracy of ±1.5% and a 
very  fast  loop  response  providing excellent performance for  dealing 
with line and load transients. The AP2112 includes an auto discharge 
function which  connects  the  output  to  ground  via  60 of  resistance 
when the device is disabled. 
 
The regulator features low power consumption, and provides SOT25, 
SOT89-5,  and  SO-8  packages. Previously  SOT-23-5,  SOT-89-5  and 
SOIC-8 packages were respectively identified as SOT23-5, SOT89-5 
and  SO-8 but  have been  renamed  to  match  the  latest  Diodes 
Incorporated‘s nomenclature. 
 
Features 
 Output Voltage Accuracy: ±1.5% 
 Output Current: 600mA (Min.) 
 Foldback Short Current Protection: 50mA 
 Enable Function to Turn ON/OFF V
OUT
 
 Low Dropout Voltage (3.3V): 250mV (Typ.) @I
OUT 
= 600mA 
 Excellent Load Regulation: 0.2%/A (Typ.) 
 Excellent Line Regulation: 0.02%/V (Typ.) 
 Low Quiescent Current: 55μA (Typ.) 
 Low Standby Current: 0.01μA (Typ.) 
 Low Output Noise: 50μV
RMS

 OTSD Protection

Aluminum Electrolytic 
 Operation Temperature Range: -40°C to +85°C 
 ESD: MM 400V, HBM 4000V  
 Totally Lead-Free & Fully RoHS Compliant (Notes 1 & 2)

Applications

STEP 4 — BUILD THE CIRCUIT
Circuit JSON format:
{
  "components": [
    { "id": "u1", "slug": "AP2112", "symType": "ic", "designator": "U1", "value": "AP2112", "x": 320, "y": 240, "rotation": 0 },
    { "id": "c1", "slug": "CAPACITOR", "symType": "capacitor", "designator": "C1", "value": "100nF", "x": 200, "y": 240, "rotation": 0 }
  ],
  "wires": [
    { "id": "w1", "points": [{"x": 280, "y": 240}, {"x": 220, "y": 240}] }
  ],
  "labels": [
    { "id": "lbl1", "name": "VCC", "x": 320, "y": 120, "rotation": 0 }
  ]
}

SLUG NAMES: "CAPACITOR", "INDUCTOR", "RESISTOR", "VCC", "GND" — IC slug: "AP2112"

COORDINATE RULES:
- Grid: 20 units. All x/y must be multiples of 20.
- Range: x 80–600, y 60–500
- Wires use L-routing (add elbow point)
- VCC rotation:0 (up), GND rotation:0 (down)
- Passive ports: rotation:0 → top (0,-20) / bottom (0,+20); rotation:1 → left (-20,0) / right (+20,0)

APPLICATION CIRCUITS FROM PROFILE:
- Typical Application Circuit (p.2): Power path: VIN source → CIN (1μF ceramic, VIN to GND) → VIN pin (SOT25 pin 1) → [AP2112] → VOUT pin (SOT25 pin 5) → COUT (1μF ceramic, VOUT to GND) → load. Enable path: VEN logic signal → EN pin (SOT25 pin 3); tie EN to VIN for always-on operation. When EN is HIGH regulator is ON; when EN is LOW regulator is OFF and VOUT discharges to GND via internal 60Ω resistor.

REQUIRED PASSIVES (minimum set from profile — EVB BOM may have MORE):
- capacitor 1.0μF ceramic: VIN to GND, as close to VIN pin as possible — CIN — input bypass capacitor. Required for stability and transient response. Recommended X7R or X5R dielectric.
- capacitor 1.0μF ceramic: VOUT to GND, as close to VOUT pin as possible — COUT — output bypass capacitor. Required for stability. Stable with ceramic, tantalum, and aluminum electrolytic. Recommended X7R or X5R dielectric if ceramic.

ALL PINS (5 total):
  Pin 1 VIN [power]
  Pin 2 GND [gnd]
  Pin 3 EN [input]
  Pin 4 NC [passive]
  Pin 5 VOUT [output]

RULES:
- Every pin used in the datasheet app circuit must be connected
- Include ALL components from the EVB BOM / Typical Application — do not skip any
- Use net labels for power/ground instead of running wires across the schematic
- Wire endpoints must hit exact port positions (center + port offset after rotation)
- All IDs must be unique strings
- After building, count your components vs the BOM — they should match

ADDITIONAL INSTRUCTIONS FROM USER:
GND shows connected to enable where it should not. When doing the layout example, the components needs to be close to each other but not overlapping.

WHEN DONE — mark this ticket complete:
PUT http://localhost:3030/api/gen-tickets/14
Body: { "status": "done" }

=======================================================

Save it with: PUT http://localhost:3030/api/library/AP2112/example_circuit

AP2112 
Document number: DS39724  Rev. 2 - 2 
1 of 18 
www.diodes.com  
June 2017 
© Diodes Incorporated 
 
AP2112 
 
600mA CMOS LDO REGULATOR WITH ENABLE 
 
Description 
The  AP2112  is  CMOS  process  low  dropout  linear  regulator  with 
enable  function,  the  regulator  delivers  a  guaranteed  600mA  (min.) 
continuous load current.  
 
The  AP2112 is  available  with  a  fixed output  voltage  of 1.2V,  1.8V, 
2.5V, 2.6V, or 3.3V. The LDO has an output accuracy of ±1.5% and a 
very  fast  loop  response  providing excellent performance for  dealing 
with line and load transients. The AP2112 includes an auto discharge 
function which  connects  the  output  to  ground  via  60 of  resistance 
when the device is disabled. 
 
The regulator features low power consumption, and provides SOT25, 
SOT89-5,  and  SO-8  packages. Previously  SOT-23-5,  SOT-89-5  and 
SOIC-8 packages were respectively identified as SOT23-5, SOT89-5 
and  SO-8 but  have been  renamed  to  match  the  latest  Diodes 
Incorporated‘s nomenclature. 
 
Features 
 Output Voltage Accuracy: ±1.5% 
 Output Current: 600mA (Min.) 
 Foldback Short Current Protection: 50mA 
 Enable Function to Turn ON/OFF V
OUT
 
 Low Dropout Voltage (3.3V): 250mV (Typ.) @I
OUT 
= 600mA 
 Excellent Load Regulation: 0.2%/A (Typ.) 
 Excellent Line Regulation: 0.02%/V (Typ.) 
 Low Quiescent Current: 55μA (Typ.) 
 Low Standby Current: 0.01μA (Typ.) 
 Low Output Noise: 50μV
RMS
 
 PSRR: 100Hz -65dB, 1kHz -65dB 
 OTSD Protection 
 Stable   with   1.0μF   Flexible   Cap:   Ceramic,   Tantalum   and 
Aluminum Electrolytic 
 Operation Temperature Range: -40°C to +85°C 
 ESD: MM 400V, HBM 4000V  
 Totally Lead-Free & Fully RoHS Compliant (Notes 1 & 2) 
 Halogen and Antimony Free. “Green” Device (Note 3)  
 
Applications 
 Laptop Computer

STEP 4 — BUILD THE CIRCUIT
Circuit JSON format:
{
  "components": [
    { "id": "u1", "slug": "AP2112", "symType": "ic", "designator": "U1", "value": "AP2112", "x": 320, "y": 240, "rotation": 0 },
    { "id": "c1", "slug": "CAPACITOR", "symType": "capacitor", "designator": "C1", "value": "100nF", "x": 200, "y": 240, "rotation": 0 }
  ],
  "wires": [
    { "id": "w1", "points": [{"x": 280, "y": 240}, {"x": 220, "y": 240}] }
  ],
  "labels": [
    { "id": "lbl1", "name": "VCC", "x": 320, "y": 120, "rotation": 0 }
  ]
}

SLUG NAMES: "CAPACITOR", "INDUCTOR", "RESISTOR", "VCC", "GND" — IC slug: "AP2112"

COORDINATE RULES:
- Grid: 20 units. All x/y must be multiples of 20.
- Range: x 80–600, y 60–500
- Wires use L-routing (add elbow point)
- VCC rotation:0 (up), GND rotation:0 (down)
- Passive ports: rotation:0 → top (0,-20) / bottom (0,+20); rotation:1 → left (-20,0) / right (+20,0)

APPLICATION CIRCUITS FROM PROFILE:
- Typical Application Circuit (p.2): Power path: VIN source → CIN (1μF ceramic, VIN to GND) → VIN pin (SOT25 pin 1) → [AP2112] → VOUT pin (SOT25 pin 5) → COUT (1μF ceramic, VOUT to GND) → load. Enable path: VEN logic signal → EN pin (SOT25 pin 3); tie EN to VIN for always-on operation. When EN is HIGH regulator is ON; when EN is LOW regulator is OFF and VOUT discharges to GND via internal 60Ω resistor.

REQUIRED PASSIVES (minimum set from profile — EVB BOM may have MORE):
- capacitor 1.0μF ceramic: VIN to GND, as close to VIN pin as possible — CIN — input bypass capacitor. Required for stability and transient response. Recommended X7R or X5R dielectric.
- capacitor 1.0μF ceramic: VOUT to GND, as close to VOUT pin as possible — COUT — output bypass capacitor. Required for stability. Stable with ceramic, tantalum, and aluminum electrolytic. Recommended X7R or X5R dielectric if ceramic.

ALL PINS (5 total):
  Pin 1 VIN [power]
  Pin 2 GND [gnd]
  Pin 3 EN [input]
  Pin 4 NC [passive]
  Pin 5 VOUT [output]

RULES:
- Every pin used in the datasheet app circuit must be connected
- Include ALL components from the EVB BOM / Typical Application — do not skip any
- Use net labels for power/ground instead of running wires across the schematic
- Wire endpoints must hit exact port positions (center + port offset after rotation)
- All IDs must be unique strings
- After building, count your components vs the BOM — they should match

ADDITIONAL INSTRUCTIONS FROM USER:
GND cannot be connected to EN/VIN pin. move it in a way that the ground symbol won't overlap with the VIN symbol

WHEN DONE — mark this ticket complete:
PUT http://localhost:3030/api/gen-tickets/15
Body: { "status": "done" }

tickets.title: Library → Component → PMA3-83LNW+ → Layout Example
tickets.prompt:
BUILD PCB LAYOUT EXAMPLE — PMA3-83LNW+
=======================================================

GOAL: Build a PCB layout for PMA3-83LNW+ that matches the datasheet's recommended land pattern and layout guidelines.
Save with: PUT http://localhost:3030/api/library/PMA3_83LNW/layout_example

STEP 1 — PARSE DATASHEET
DATASHEET TEXT — find the "Land Pattern", "PCB Layout", "Recommended Layout", or "Mechanical" section:


www.minicircuits.com    P.O. Box 350166, Brooklyn, NY 11235-0003    (718) 934-4500    sales@minicircuits.comwww.minicircuits.com    P.O. Box 350166, Brooklyn, NY 11235-0003    (718) 934-4500    sales@minicircuits.com
PAGE 1 OF 5
Monolithic Amplifier
LOW NOISE, WIDEBAND, HIGH IP3
PMA3-83LNW+
PRODUCT OVERVIEW
The PMA3-83LNW+ is a PHEMT based wideband, low noise MMIC amplifier with a unique combination of low noise, high IP3, 
and flat gain over wideband making it ideal for sensitive, high-dynamic-range receiver applications.  This design operates on a 
single 5V or 6V supply, is well matched for 50Ω and comes in a tiny, low profile package (3 x 3 x 0.89mm), accommodating dense 
circuit board layouts.
FeatureAdvantages
Low noise, 1.2 dB at 2 GHzEnables lower system noise figure performance.
High IP3
 y+37 dBm at 2 GHz
 y+29 dBm at 8 GHz
Combination of low noise and high IP3 makes this MMIC amplifier ideal for use in low noise receiver front end 
(RFE) as it gives the user advantages of sensitivity and two-tone IM performance at both ends of the dynamic 
range.
Low operating voltage, 5V/6V.Achieves high IP3 using low voltage.
3 x 3mm 12-lead MCLP package
Tiny footprint saves space in dense layouts while providing low inductance, repeatable transitions, and excellent 
thermal contact to the PCB.
Wide bandwidth with flat gain
 y±0.6 dB over 0.4 to 7 GHz
 y±1.5 dB over 0.4 to 8 GHz
Enables a single amplifier to be used in many wideband applications including defense, instrumentation and more.
KEY FEATURES
50Ω      0.4 to 8.0 GHz
REV. A
ECO-010881
PMA3-83LNW+
MCL NY
211201
APPLICATIONS
 yWiFi
 yWLAN
 yUMTS

    }
  ],

RULES:
- Component center at roughly board center
- Add 100nF–1µF decoupling cap for each VCC/VDD pin — place within 2mm of the pin
- Board size should be just large enough for the component + decoupling caps + clearance
- Use the pad positions from the assigned footprint (x/y relative to component center)
- Net names: match datasheet pin names for power/signal nets
- Keep traces short for decoupling caps
- Power/supply pins on this component: GND (Paddle)

COMPONENT ORIENTATION RULES — follow these every time:
1. Orient each cap so ONE PAD aligns exactly (same X or same Y) with the IC pin it connects to.
   This allows a single straight horizontal or vertical trace segment — no dog-legs needed.
2. Place the cap's connecting pad on the side FACING the IC pin.
   Example: IC VIN at (6.05, 5.45), cap to the left → cap rotation=0, pad2 (right) at (comp.x+0.5, comp.y).
   Set comp.y = 5.45 and comp.x so pad2 is just outside U1 courtyard. Trace: straight horizontal.
3. The non-connecting pad (GND or supply return) faces away from the IC.

TRACE ROUTING RULES — follow these every time:
1. Check every trace pair for crossings BEFORE writing the JSON. Different-net traces must not cross.
2. Route GND back-traces on a side that avoids crossing signal/power traces.
   Prefer routing GND going toward the outer board edge, away from signal traces.
3. For EN-to-VIN ties: route the loop on the OPPOSITE side of the IC from the signal traces to avoid crossings.
4. Never route a trace at the same Y as an IC pin row through a pad of a different net.
5. Plan the full trace layout mentally before writing: list all trace paths, then check each pair for intersections.

45° ANGLE ROUTING — always prefer 45° bends over 90°:
- Never use 90° bends. Use 45° diagonal segments to change direction instead.
- At every corner: go straight first, then add a short 45° segment, then go straight again.
  Example corner turning left: vert segment → 45° NW diagonal → horiz segment (not vert→horiz directly).
- Copper clearance applies at angles too. Verify every diagonal segment clears all pads of different nets.
  Minimum copper gap = ptSegDist(pad_center, seg_start, seg_end) − trace_half_width − pad_radius ≥ dr.clearance.
- If a 45° diagonal is too close to a pad, first extend the straight segment further away before turning.

CLOSEST-VALID-POINT ROUTING — always route to the nearest pad of the same net:
- When a pin needs to connect to a net that has multiple connection points, route to the NEAREST one.
  Example: EN (tied to VIN) should route to the nearest VIN point (U1.pad1), not to C1.pad2 which is farther.
  The daisy-chain then continues from U1.pad1 → C1.pad2 naturally through the VIN trace.
- Check all pads of the target net and pick the pad with shortest trace path (not just Euclidean distance —
  account for obstacles and routing constraints).

PRODUCT OVERVIEW
The PMA3-83LNW+ is a PHEMT based wideband, low noise MMIC amplifier with a unique combination of low noise, high IP3, 
and flat gain over wideband making it ideal for sensitive, high-dynamic-range receiver applications.  This design operates on a 
single 5V or 6V supply, is well matched for 50Ω and comes in a tiny, low profile package (3 x 3 x 0.89mm), accommodating dense 
circuit board layouts.

range.
Low operating voltage, 5V/6V.Achieves high IP3 using low voltage.
3 x 3mm 12-lead MCLP package

BUILD PCB LAYOUT EXAMPLE — PMA3-83LNW+
=======================================================

GOAL: Build a PCB layout for PMA3-83LNW+ that matches the datasheet's recommended land pattern and layout guidelines.
Save with: PUT http://localhost:3030/api/library/PMA3_83LNW/layout_example

Power/supply pins on this component: GND (Paddle)

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
MANDATORY RULES — READ BEFORE DOING ANYTHING ELSE
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PCB Layout Example — Agent Instructions

**Read this file completely before writing any JSON. Every rule here is mandatory.**

These rules apply every time a `layout` gen-ticket is executed. Violating them produces boards that fail DRC and have to be redone from scratch.

---

## Step 0 — Read this file first

This is step 0. Do it before reading the datasheet, before loading the footprint, before writing a single coordinate.

---

## Step 1 — Identify ALL components before placing anything

Before computing a single coordinate, list every component that must appear in the layout:

1. **The IC itself** (U1)
2. **Every decoupling/bypass cap** required by the datasheet — one 100nF–1µF cap per VCC/VDD/supply pin
3. **Every inductor** required by the datasheet (bias chokes, RF chokes, etc.)
4. **Every resistor** required (pull-ups, current limiting, bias networks)
5. **Every component shown in the datasheet's "Typical Application" or "Recommended Layout" schematic**

**All of these must appear in the `components` array.** A layout that omits any required component is wrong — do not save it.

Write your component list here before placing:
```
U1  — main IC
C1  — 100nF bypass on VIN (pad X)
C2  — 100nF bypass on VCC (pad Y)
L1  — RF choke on RF-OUT (if required)
...
```
Do not proceed to coordinate calculation until the list is complete.

---

## Package dimensions reference

Every footprint has a **courtyard** — the keep-out zone around it. No courtyard of one component may overlap the courtyard of another. Use these dimensions every time you compute placement coordinates.

### IC / active packages

| Footprint | Body | Courtyard (half-width × half-height) | Pad outer edge |
|-----------|------|--------------------------------------|----------------|
| QFN-12-3x3 | 3×3 mm | ±2.0 mm × ±2.0 mm | ±2.1 mm (X/Y) |
| QFN-16-3x3 | 3×3 mm | ±2.45 mm × ±2.45 mm | ±2.15 mm (X/Y) |
| MCLP-4 | 1.65×1.3 mm | ±1.4 mm × ±1.2 mm | ±1.075 mm (X), ±0.815 mm (Y) |
| MCLP-8 | 1.65×1.3 mm | ±1.4 mm × ±1.1 mm | ±1.075 mm (X), ±0.815 mm (Y) |
| MCLP-12 | 3.2×2.8 mm | ±1.6 mm × ±1.4 mm | ±1.275 mm (X), ±1.125 mm (Y) |
| DFN-6-1.8x1.8 | 1.8×1.8 mm | ±1.3 mm × ±1.225 mm | ±1.0 mm (X), ±0.775 mm (Y) |

---

## Minimum safe center-to-center spacing

**Formula:** `min_distance = IC_courtyard_half + cap_courtyard_half + 0.25 mm safety margin`

The 0.25 mm margin ensures DRC clearance is never violated by rounding errors.

### QFN-12-3x3 + 0402 (most common combination)

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal (side-by-side) | 2.0 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 3.25 mm** |
| Vertical (above/below) | 2.0 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.75 mm** |

Example: IC at x=12.5. Cap placed to the right → `cap.x ≥ 12.5 + 3.25 = 15.75`. Use 16.0.
Example: IC at y=10.0. Cap placed below → `cap.y ≥ 10.0 + 2.75 = 12.75`. Use 13.0.

### MCLP-4 + 0402

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal | 1.4 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.65 mm** |
| Vertical | 1.2 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 1.95 mm** |

### MCLP-8 + 0402

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal | 1.4 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.65 mm** |
| Vertical | 1.1 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 1.85 mm** |

### General rule for any combination
```
min_center_distance_X = IC_cy_half_X + passive_cy_half_X + 0.25
min_center_distance_Y = IC_cy_half_Y + passive_cy_half_Y + 0.25
```
Look up the courtyard half values in the tables above.

---

## General layout rules

- Place the IC component center at roughly the board center
- Add a 100nF–1µF decoupling cap for each VCC/VDD pin — place it within 2 mm of the pin
- Board size should be just large enough for the component + decoupling caps + clearance — do not add empty space
- Use the pad positions from the assigned footprint (x/y are relative to the component center at 0,0)
- Net names: match the schematic net names where available; use datasheet signal names (VIN, GND, EN…) otherwise
- Keep decoupling-cap traces as short as possible

---

## Component orientation rules — follow every time

### Rule 1 — Single-axis alignment
Orient each decoupling cap so that **one pad aligns exactly** (same X or same Y coordinate) with the IC pin it connects to.
This allows a single straight horizontal or vertical trace segment — no dog-legs, no bends.

### Rule 2 — Connecting pad faces the IC
Place the cap's connecting pad on the **side facing the IC pin**.

Example: IC VIN pin is at world coordinate (6.05, 5.45). You want to place a cap to its left.
- Set `comp.y = 5.45` (same Y as VIN pin)
- Set `comp.rotation = 0` → pad2 is the right-side pad at `(comp.x + 0.5, comp.y)`
- Set `comp.x` so pad2 sits just outside the IC courtyard — use the spacing table above
- Trace: one straight horizontal segment from VIN pad to cap pad2

### Rule 3 — GND pad faces away
The non-connecting pad (GND or supply return) must face **away** from the IC, toward the board edge. This keeps the GND trace clear of the IC.

---

## Trace routing rules — follow every time

### Rule 1 — No crossings
Check every trace pair for crossings **before writing the JSON**. Different-net traces must not cross. Plan the full layout mentally first — list all trace paths, then check each pair for intersections.

### Rule 2 — GND routing direction
Route GND back-traces on a side that avoids crossing signal/power traces.
Prefer routing GND toward the outer board edge, away from signal traces.

### Rule 3 — EN-to-VIN ties
When EN is tied to VIN, route that loop on the **opposite side** of the IC from the signal traces to avoid crossings.

### Rule 4 — No trace through a foreign pad
Never route a trace at the same Y (or X) as a pad row if that trace belongs to a different net than the pad.

### Rule 5 — Plan before writing
Write out all trace paths as scratch notes before committing to JSON. Verify each pair (A×B) for intersections before writing.

---

## 45° angle routing — always prefer 45° bends, never 90°

- **Never** use 90° bends (horizontal segment directly connected to vertical segment).
- Use a short 45° diagonal to change direction at every corner.
- Pattern: straight segment → 45° diagonal → straight segment.
  - Example turning left: vertical segment → short NW diagonal → horizontal segment.
- Copper clearance applies to diagonals too.
  Verify: `ptSegDist(pad_center, seg_start, seg_end) − trace_half_width − pad_radius ≥ dr.clearance`
- If a 45° diagonal is too close to a pad, extend the straight segment further before turning.

---

## Closest-valid-point routing

When connecting a pin to a net that already has multiple pads/connection points, **route to the nearest existing point**, not to an arbitrary pad of that net.

Example: EN needs to connect to VIN. VIN exists on U1.pad1 (1 mm away) and C1.pad2 (4 mm away).
→ Route EN → U1.pad1 (nearest). Let the existing VIN trace carry the connection to C1.pad2.
Do not create a long trace to a far pad when a closer tap point exists.

Always compute Euclidean distance to each pad of the target net and route to the minimum, adjusted for any obstacles.

---

## Board JSON format

All dimensions in mm. Use this exact structure:

```json
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "title": "<PART_NUMBER> Layout Example",
  "board": { "width": 25, "height": 20, "units": "mm" },
  "components": [
    {
      "id": "U1",
      "ref": "U1",
      "value": "<PART_NUMBER>",
      "footprint": "<footprint-name>",
      "x": 12.5,
      "y": 10,
      "rotation": 0,
      "layer": "F",
      "pads": [
        {
          "number": "1",
          "name": "PIN1",
          "x": -1.0,
          "y": 0,
          "type": "smd",
          "shape": "rect",
          "size_x": 0.6,
          "size_y": 1.5,
          "net": "VIN"
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "nets": [
    { "name": "VIN", "pads": ["U1.1"] }
  ],
  "traces": [
    {
      "net": "VIN",
      "segments": [
        { "start": { "x": 11.5, "y": 10 }, "end": { "x": 10.5, "y": 10 } }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "vias": []
}
```

Key rules for the JSON:
- `pads[].x` and `pads[].y` are **relative to the component center**, not world coordinates
- `traces[].segments[].start/end` are **world coordinates** (mm from board origin)
- `rotation` is degrees clockwise; 0 = default orientation per footprint
- Every pad that carries a signal must have a non-empty `net` string
- Every net in `nets[]` must list all pad references (`"REFDES.padnumber"`) that belong to it

---

## Self-check before saving

Before calling `PUT .../layout_example`, verify every item on this list:

1. **All components present** — every IC, cap, inductor, resistor from the required list is in `components[]`
2. **No courtyard overlaps** — every component pair satisfies the minimum spacing from the tables above
3. **No 90° bends** — every direction change uses a 45° segment
4. **No trace crossings** — every different-net trace pair is non-intersecting
5. **Cap alignment** — each cap's connecting pad shares X or Y with its IC pin
6. **Cap orientation** — connecting pad faces the IC, GND pad faces away
7. **Closest routing** — each pin routes to the nearest pad of its net
8. **All pads have nets** — no pad on a signal pin has an empty `net` field
9. **Board size fits** — all components + 1 mm margin inside board boundary

# PCB Layout Example — Agent Instructions

**Read this file completely before writing any JSON. Every rule here is mandatory.**

These rules apply every time a `layout` gen-ticket is executed. Violating them produces boards that fail DRC and have to be redone from scratch.

---

## Step 0 — Read this file first

This is step 0. Do it before reading the datasheet, before loading the footprint, before writing a single coordinate.

---

## Step 1 — Identify ALL components before placing anything

Before computing a single coordinate, list every component that must appear in the layout:

1. **The IC itself** (U1)
2. **Every decoupling/bypass cap** required by the datasheet — one 100nF–1µF cap per VCC/VDD/supply pin
3. **Every inductor** required by the datasheet (bias chokes, RF chokes, etc.)
4. **Every resistor** required (pull-ups, current limiting, bias networks)
5. **Every component shown in the datasheet's "Typical Application" or "Recommended Layout" schematic**

**All of these must appear in the `components` array.** A layout that omits any required component is wrong — do not save it.

Write your component list here before placing:
```
U1  — main IC
C1  — 100nF bypass on VIN (pad X)
C2  — 100nF bypass on VCC (pad Y)
L1  — RF choke on RF-OUT (if required)
...
```
Do not proceed to coordinate calculation until the list is complete.

## Minimum safe center-to-center spacing

**Formula:** `min_distance = IC_courtyard_half + cap_courtyard_half + 0.25 mm safety margin`

The 0.25 mm margin ensures DRC clearance is never violated by rounding errors.

### QFN-12-3x3 + 0402 (most common combination)

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal (side-by-side) | 2.0 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 3.25 mm** |
| Vertical (above/below) | 2.0 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.75 mm** |

Example: IC at x=12.5. Cap placed to the right → `cap.x ≥ 12.5 + 3.25 = 15.75`. Use 16.0.
Example: IC at y=10.0. Cap placed below → `cap.y ≥ 10.0 + 2.75 = 12.75`. Use 13.0.

### MCLP-4 + 0402

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal | 1.4 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.65 mm** |
| Vertical | 1.2 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 1.95 mm** |

### MCLP-8 + 0402

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal | 1.4 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.65 mm** |
| Vertical | 1.1 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 1.85 mm** |

### General rule for any combination
```
min_center_distance_X = IC_cy_half_X + passive_cy_half_X + 0.25
min_center_distance_Y = IC_cy_half_Y + passive_cy_half_Y + 0.25
```
Look up the courtyard half values in the tables above.

---

## General layout rules

- Place the IC component center at roughly the board center
- Add a 100nF–1µF decoupling cap for each VCC/VDD pin — place it within 2 mm of the pin
- Board size should be just large enough for the component + decoupling caps + clearance — do not add empty space
- Use the pad positions from the assigned footprint (x/y are relative to the component center at 0,0)
- Net names: match the schematic net names where available; use datasheet signal names (VIN, GND, EN…) otherwise
- Keep decoupling-cap traces as short as possible

---

## Component orientation rules — follow every time

### Rule 1 — Single-axis alignment
Orient each decoupling cap so that **one pad aligns exactly** (same X or same Y coordinate) with the IC pin it connects to.
This allows a single straight horizontal or vertical trace segment — no dog-legs, no bends.

### Rule 2 — Connecting pad faces the IC
Place the cap's connecting pad on the **side facing the IC pin**.

Example: IC VIN pin is at world coordinate (6.05, 5.45). You want to place a cap to its left.
- Set `comp.y = 5.45` (same Y as VIN pin)
- Set `comp.rotation = 0` → pad2 is the right-side pad at `(comp.x + 0.5, comp.y)`
- Set `comp.x` so pad2 sits just outside the IC courtyard — use the spacing table above
- Trace: one straight horizontal segment from VIN pad to cap pad2

### Rule 3 — GND pad faces away
The non-connecting pad (GND or supply return) must face **away** from the IC, toward the board edge. This keeps the GND trace clear of the IC.

---

## Trace routing rules — follow every time

### Rule 1 — No crossings
Check every trace pair for crossings **before writing the JSON**. Different-net traces must not cross. Plan the full layout mentally first — list all trace paths, then check each pair for intersections.

### Rule 2 — GND routing direction
Route GND back-traces on a side that avoids crossing signal/power traces.
Prefer routing GND toward the outer board edge, away from signal traces.

### Rule 3 — EN-to-VIN ties
When EN is tied to VIN, route that loop on the **opposite side** of the IC from the signal traces to avoid crossings.

### Rule 4 — No trace through a foreign pad
Never route a trace at the same Y (or X) as a pad row if that trace belongs to a different net than the pad.

### Rule 5 — Plan before writing
Write out all trace paths as scratch notes before committing to JSON. Verify each pair (A×B) for intersections before writing.

---

## 45° angle routing — always prefer 45° bends, never 90°

- **Never** use 90° bends (horizontal segment directly connected to vertical segment).
- Use a short 45° diagonal to change direction at every corner.
- Pattern: straight segment → 45° diagonal → straight segment.
  - Example turning left: vertical segment → short NW diagonal → horizontal segment.
- Copper clearance applies to diagonals too.
  Verify: `ptSegDist(pad_center, seg_start, seg_end) − trace_half_width − pad_radius ≥ dr.clearance`
- If a 45° diagonal is too close to a pad, extend the straight segment further before turning.

---

## Closest-valid-point routing

When connecting a pin to a net that already has multiple pads/connection points, **route to the nearest existing point**, not to an arbitrary pad of that net.

Example: EN needs to connect to VIN. VIN exists on U1.pad1 (1 mm away) and C1.pad2 (4 mm away).
→ Route EN → U1.pad1 (nearest). Let the existing VIN trace carry the connection to C1.pad2.
Do not create a long trace to a far pad when a closer tap point exists.

Always compute Euclidean distance to each pad of the target net and route to the minimum, adjusted for any obstacles.

---

## Board JSON format

All dimensions in mm. Use this exact structure:

```json
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "title": "<PART_NUMBER> Layout Example",
  "board": { "width": 25, "height": 20, "units": "mm" },
  "components": [
    {
      "id": "U1",
      "ref": "U1",
      "value": "<PART_NUMBER>",
      "footprint": "<footprint-name>",
      "x": 12.5,
      "y": 10,
      "rotation": 0,
      "layer": "F",
      "pads": [
        {
          "number": "1",
          "name": "PIN1",
          "x": -1.0,
          "y": 0,
          "type": "smd",
          "shape": "rect",
          "size_x": 0.6,
          "size_y": 1.5,
          "net": "VIN"
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "nets": [
    { "name": "VIN", "pads": ["U1.1"] }
  ],
  "traces": [
    {
      "net": "VIN",
      "segments": [
        { "start": { "x": 11.5, "y": 10 }, "end": { "x": 10.5, "y": 10 } }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "vias": []
}
```

Key rules for the JSON:
- `pads[].x` and `pads[].y` are **relative to the component center**, not world coordinates
- `traces[].segments[].start/end` are **world coordinates** (mm from board origin)
- `rotation` is degrees clockwise; 0 = default orientation per footprint
- Every pad that carries a signal must have a non-empty `net` string
- Every net in `nets[]` must list all pad references (`"REFDES.padnumber"`) that belong to it

---

## Self-check before saving

Before calling `PUT .../layout_example`, verify every item on this list:

1. **All components present** — every IC, cap, inductor, resistor from the required list is in `components[]`
2. **No courtyard overlaps** — every component pair satisfies the minimum spacing from the tables above
3. **No 90° bends** — every direction change uses a 45° segment
4. **No trace crossings** — every different-net trace pair is non-intersecting
5. **Cap alignment** — each cap's connecting pad shares X or Y with its IC pin
6. **Cap orientation** — connecting pad faces the IC, GND pad faces away
7. **Closest routing** — each pin routes to the nearest pad of its net
8. **All pads have nets** — no pad on a signal pin has an empty `net` field
9. **Board size fits** — all components + 1 mm margin inside board boundary

# PCB Layout Example — Agent Instructions

**Read this file completely before writing any JSON. Every rule here is mandatory.**

These rules apply every time a `layout` gen-ticket is executed. Violating them produces boards that fail DRC and have to be redone from scratch.

---

## Step 0 — Clear existing layout first

Before doing anything else, wipe the existing layout so you are building on a clean slate:

```
PUT http://localhost:3030/api/library/<slug>/layout_example
Content-Type: application/json

null
```

This prevents any old component positions, traces, or nets from polluting the new design. Do not skip this step — old data causes the reviewer to think the new layout is good when it may have been partially inherited from a broken prior version.

---

## Step 1 — Read this file completely

Do this before reading the datasheet, before loading the footprint, before writing a single coordinate.

---

## Step 2 — Identify ALL components before placing anything

Before computing a single coordinate, list every component that must appear in the layout:

1. **The IC itself** (U1)
2. **Every decoupling/bypass cap** required by the datasheet — one 100nF–1µF cap per VCC/VDD/supply pin
3. **Every inductor** required by the datasheet (bias chokes, RF chokes, etc.)
4. **Every resistor** required (pull-ups, current limiting, bias networks)
5. **Every component shown in the datasheet's "Typical Application" or "Recommended Layout" schematic**

**All of these must appear in the `components` array.** A layout that omits any required component is wrong — do not save it.

Write your component list here before placing:
```
U1  — main IC
C1  — 100nF bypass on VIN (pad X)
C2  — 100nF bypass on VCC (pad Y)
L1  — RF choke on RF-OUT (if required)
...
```
Do not proceed to coordinate calculation until the list is complete.

---

## Minimum safe center-to-center spacing

**Formula:** `min_distance = IC_courtyard_half + cap_courtyard_half + 0.25 mm safety margin`

The 0.25 mm margin ensures DRC clearance is never violated by rounding errors.

### QFN-12-3x3 + 0402 (most common combination)

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal (side-by-side) | 2.0 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 3.25 mm** |
| Vertical (above/below) | 2.0 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.75 mm** |

Example: IC at x=12.5. Cap placed to the right → `cap.x ≥ 12.5 + 3.25 = 15.75`. Use 16.0.
Example: IC at y=10.0. Cap placed below → `cap.y ≥ 10.0 + 2.75 = 12.75`. Use 13.0.

### MCLP-4 + 0402

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal | 1.4 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.65 mm** |
| Vertical | 1.2 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 1.95 mm** |

### MCLP-8 + 0402

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal | 1.4 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.65 mm** |
| Vertical | 1.1 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 1.85 mm** |

### General rule for any combination
```
min_center_distance_X = IC_cy_half_X + passive_cy_half_X + 0.25
min_center_distance_Y = IC_cy_half_Y + passive_cy_half_Y + 0.25
```
Look up the courtyard half values in the tables above.

---

## General layout rules

- Place the IC component center at roughly the board center
- Add a 100nF–1µF decoupling cap for each VCC/VDD pin — place it within 2 mm of the pin
- Board size should be just large enough for the component + decoupling caps + clearance — do not add empty space
- Use the pad positions from the assigned footprint (x/y are relative to the component center at 0,0)
- Net names: match the schematic net names where available; use datasheet signal names (VIN, GND, EN…) otherwise
- Keep decoupling-cap traces as short as possible

---

## Component orientation rules — follow every time

### Rule 1 — Single-axis alignment
Orient each decoupling cap so that **one pad aligns exactly** (same X or same Y coordinate) with the IC pin it connects to.
This allows a single straight horizontal or vertical trace segment — no dog-legs, no bends.

### Rule 2 — Connecting pad faces the IC
Place the cap's connecting pad on the **side facing the IC pin**.

Example: IC VIN pin is at world coordinate (6.05, 5.45). You want to place a cap to its left.
- Set `comp.y = 5.45` (same Y as VIN pin)
- Set `comp.rotation = 0` → pad2 is the right-side pad at `(comp.x + 0.5, comp.y)`
- Set `comp.x` so pad2 sits just outside the IC courtyard — use the spacing table above
- Trace: one straight horizontal segment from VIN pad to cap pad2

### Rule 3 — GND pad faces away
The non-connecting pad (GND or supply return) must face **away** from the IC, toward the board edge. This keeps the GND trace clear of the IC.

---

## Trace routing rules — follow every time

### Rule 1 — No crossings
Check every trace pair for crossings **before writing the JSON**. Different-net traces must not cross. Plan the full layout mentally first — list all trace paths, then check each pair for intersections.

### Rule 2 — GND routing direction
Route GND back-traces on a side that avoids crossing signal/power traces.
Prefer routing GND toward the outer board edge, away from signal traces.

### Rule 3 — EN-to-VIN ties
When EN is tied to VIN, route that loop on the **opposite side** of the IC from the signal traces to avoid crossings.

### Rule 4 — No trace through a foreign pad
Never route a trace at the same Y (or X) as a pad row if that trace belongs to a different net than the pad.

### Rule 5 — Plan before writing
Write out all trace paths as scratch notes before committing to JSON. Verify each pair (A×B) for intersections before writing.

---

## 45° angle routing — always prefer 45° bends, never 90°

- **Never** use 90° bends (horizontal segment directly connected to vertical segment).
- Use a short 45° diagonal to change direction at every corner.
- Pattern: straight segment → 45° diagonal → straight segment.
  - Example turning left: vertical segment → short NW diagonal → horizontal segment.
- Copper clearance applies to diagonals too.
  Verify: `ptSegDist(pad_center, seg_start, seg_end) − trace_half_width − pad_radius ≥ dr.clearance`
- If a 45° diagonal is too close to a pad, extend the straight segment further before turning.

---

## Closest-valid-point routing

When connecting a pin to a net that already has multiple pads/connection points, **route to the nearest existing point**, not to an arbitrary pad of that net.

Example: EN needs to connect to VIN. VIN exists on U1.pad1 (1 mm away) and C1.pad2 (4 mm away).
→ Route EN → U1.pad1 (nearest). Let the existing VIN trace carry the connection to C1.pad2.
Do not create a long trace to a far pad when a closer tap point exists.

Always compute Euclidean distance to each pad of the target net and route to the minimum, adjusted for any obstacles.

---

## Board JSON format

All dimensions in mm. Use this exact structure:

```json
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "title": "<PART_NUMBER> Layout Example",
  "board": { "width": 25, "height": 20, "units": "mm" },
  "components": [
    {
      "id": "U1",
      "ref": "U1",
      "value": "<PART_NUMBER>",
      "footprint": "<footprint-name>",
      "x": 12.5,
      "y": 10,
      "rotation": 0,
      "layer": "F",
      "pads": [
        {
          "number": "1",
          "name": "PIN1",
          "x": -1.0,
          "y": 0,
          "type": "smd",
          "shape": "rect",
          "size_x": 0.6,
          "size_y": 1.5,
          "net": "VIN"
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "nets": [
    { "name": "VIN", "pads": ["U1.1"] }
  ],
  "traces": [
    {
      "net": "VIN",
      "segments": [
        { "start": { "x": 11.5, "y": 10 }, "end": { "x": 10.5, "y": 10 } }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "vias": []
}
```

Key rules for the JSON:
- `pads[].x` and `pads[].y` are **relative to the component center**, not world coordinates
- `traces[].segments[].start/end` are **world coordinates** (mm from board origin)
- `rotation` is degrees clockwise; 0 = default orientation per footprint
- Every pad that carries a signal must have a non-empty `net` string
- Every net in `nets[]` must list all pad references (`"REFDES.padnumber"`) that belong to it

---

## Self-check before saving

Before calling `PUT .../layout_example`, verify every item on this list:

1. **All components present** — every IC, cap, inductor, resistor from the required list is in `components[]`
2. **No courtyard overlaps** — every component pair satisfies the minimum spacing from the tables above
3. **No 90° bends** — every direction change uses a 45° segment
4. **No trace crossings** — every different-net trace pair is non-intersecting
5. **Cap alignment** — each cap's connecting pad shares X or Y with its IC pin
6. **Cap orientation** — connecting pad faces the IC, GND pad faces away
7. **Closest routing** — each pin routes to the nearest pad of its net
8. **All pads have nets** — no pad on a signal pin has an empty `net` field
9. **Board size fits** — all components + 1 mm margin inside board boundary

# PCB Layout Example — Agent Instructions

**Read this file completely before writing any JSON. Every rule here is mandatory.**

These rules apply every time a `layout` gen-ticket is executed. Violating them produces boards that fail DRC and have to be redone from scratch.

---

## Step 0 — Clear existing layout first

Before doing anything else, wipe the existing layout so you are building on a clean slate:

```
DELETE http://localhost:3030/api/library/<slug>/layout_example
```

This prevents any old component positions, traces, or nets from polluting the new design. Do not skip this step — old data causes the reviewer to think the new layout is good when it may have been partially inherited from a broken prior version.

---

## Step 1 — Read this file completely

Do this before reading the datasheet, before loading the footprint, before writing a single coordinate.

---

## Step 2 — Identify ALL components before placing anything

Before computing a single coordinate, list every component that must appear in the layout:

1. **The IC itself** (U1)
2. **Every decoupling/bypass cap** required by the datasheet — one 100nF–1µF cap per VCC/VDD/supply pin
3. **Every inductor** required by the datasheet (bias chokes, RF chokes, etc.)
4. **Every resistor** required (pull-ups, current limiting, bias networks)
5. **Every component shown in the datasheet's "Typical Application" or "Recommended Layout" schematic**

**All of these must appear in the `components` array.** A layout that omits any required component is wrong — do not save it.

Write your component list here before placing:
```
U1  — main IC
C1  — 100nF bypass on VIN (pad X)
C2  — 100nF bypass on VCC (pad Y)
L1  — RF choke on RF-OUT (if required)
...
```
Do not proceed to coordinate calculation until the list is complete.

---

## Package dimensions reference

Every footprint has a **courtyard** — the keep-out zone around it. No courtyard of one component may overlap the courtyard of another. Use these dimensions every time you compute placement coordinates.

### IC / active packages

| Footprint | Body | Courtyard (half-width × half-height) | Pad outer edge |
|-----------|------|--------------------------------------|----------------|
| QFN-12-3x3 | 3×3 mm | ±2.0 mm × ±2.0 mm | ±2.1 mm (X/Y) |
| QFN-16-3x3 | 3×3 mm | ±2.45 mm × ±2.45 mm | ±2.15 mm (X/Y) |
| MCLP-4 | 1.65×1.3 mm | ±1.4 mm × ±1.2 mm | ±1.075 mm (X), ±0.815 mm (Y) |
| MCLP-8 | 1.65×1.3 mm | ±1.4 mm × ±1.1 mm | ±1.075 mm (X), ±0.815 mm (Y) |
| MCLP-12 | 3.2×2.8 mm | ±1.6 mm × ±1.4 mm | ±1.275 mm (X), ±1.125 mm (Y) |

---

## Minimum safe center-to-center spacing

**Formula:** `min_distance = IC_courtyard_half + cap_courtyard_half + 0.25 mm safety margin`

The 0.25 mm margin ensures DRC clearance is never violated by rounding errors.

### QFN-12-3x3 + 0402 (most common combination)

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal (side-by-side) | 2.0 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 3.25 mm** |
| Vertical (above/below) | 2.0 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.75 mm** |

Example: IC at x=12.5. Cap placed to the right → `cap.x ≥ 12.5 + 3.25 = 15.75`. Use 16.0.
Example: IC at y=10.0. Cap placed below → `cap.y ≥ 10.0 + 2.75 = 12.75`. Use 13.0.

### MCLP-4 + 0402

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal | 1.4 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.65 mm** |
| Vertical | 1.2 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 1.95 mm** |

### MCLP-8 + 0402

| Direction | IC half | Cap half | Safety | **Minimum** |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------|-------------|
| Horizontal | 1.4 mm | 1.0 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 2.65 mm** |
| Vertical | 1.1 mm | 0.5 mm | 0.25 mm | **≥ 1.85 mm** |

### General rule for any combination
```
min_center_distance_X = IC_cy_half_X + passive_cy_half_X + 0.25
min_center_distance_Y = IC_cy_half_Y + passive_cy_half_Y + 0.25
```
Look up the courtyard half values in the tables above.

---

## General layout rules

- Place the IC component center at roughly the board center
- Add a 100nF–1µF decoupling cap for each VCC/VDD pin — place it within 2 mm of the pin
- Board size should be just large enough for the component + decoupling caps + clearance — do not add empty space
- Use the pad positions from the assigned footprint (x/y are relative to the component center at 0,0)
- Net names: match the schematic net names where available; use datasheet signal names (VIN, GND, EN…) otherwise
- Keep decoupling-cap traces as short as possible

---

## Component orientation rules — follow every time

### Rule 1 — Single-axis alignment
Orient each decoupling cap so that **one pad aligns exactly** (same X or same Y coordinate) with the IC pin it connects to.
This allows a single straight horizontal or vertical trace segment — no dog-legs, no bends.

### Rule 2 — Connecting pad faces the IC
Place the cap's connecting pad on the **side facing the IC pin**.

Example: IC VIN pin is at world coordinate (6.05, 5.45). You want to place a cap to its left.
- Set `comp.y = 5.45` (same Y as VIN pin)
- Set `comp.rotation = 0` → pad2 is the right-side pad at `(comp.x + 0.5, comp.y)`
- Set `comp.x` so pad2 sits just outside the IC courtyard — use the spacing table above
- Trace: one straight horizontal segment from VIN pad to cap pad2

### Rule 3 — GND pad faces away
The non-connecting pad (GND or supply return) must face **away** from the IC, toward the board edge. This keeps the GND trace clear of the IC.

---

## Trace routing rules — follow every time

### Rule 1 — No crossings
Check every trace pair for crossings **before writing the JSON**. Different-net traces must not cross. Plan the full layout mentally first — list all trace paths, then check each pair for intersections.

### Rule 2 — GND routing direction
Route GND back-traces on a side that avoids crossing signal/power traces.
Prefer routing GND toward the outer board edge, away from signal traces.

### Rule 3 — EN-to-VIN ties
When EN is tied to VIN, route that loop on the **opposite side** of the IC from the signal traces to avoid crossings.

### Rule 4 — No trace through a foreign pad
Never route a trace at the same Y (or X) as a pad row if that trace belongs to a different net than the pad.

### Rule 5 — Plan before writing
Write out all trace paths as scratch notes before committing to JSON. Verify each pair (A×B) for intersections before writing.

---

## 45° angle routing — always prefer 45° bends, never 90°

- **Never** use 90° bends (horizontal segment directly connected to vertical segment).
- Use a short 45° diagonal to change direction at every corner.
- Pattern: straight segment → 45° diagonal → straight segment.
  - Example turning left: vertical segment → short NW diagonal → horizontal segment.
- Copper clearance applies to diagonals too.
  Verify: `ptSegDist(pad_center, seg_start, seg_end) − trace_half_width − pad_radius ≥ dr.clearance`
- If a 45° diagonal is too close to a pad, extend the straight segment further before turning.

---

## Closest-valid-point routing

When connecting a pin to a net that already has multiple pads/connection points, **route to the nearest existing point**, not to an arbitrary pad of that net.

Example: EN needs to connect to VIN. VIN exists on U1.pad1 (1 mm away) and C1.pad2 (4 mm away).
→ Route EN → U1.pad1 (nearest). Let the existing VIN trace carry the connection to C1.pad2.
Do not create a long trace to a far pad when a closer tap point exists.

Always compute Euclidean distance to each pad of the target net and route to the minimum, adjusted for any obstacles.

---

tickets.title: Library → Component → PMA3-83LNW+ → Layout Example
tickets.prompt:
BUILD PCB LAYOUT EXAMPLE — PMA3-83LNW+
=======================================================

GOAL: Build a complete PCB layout for PMA3-83LNW+ from scratch.
Power/supply pins: GND (Paddle)

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
MANDATORY RULES — READ BEFORE DOING ANYTHING ELSE
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PCB Layout Example — Agent Instructions

---

## Step 0 — Clear existing layout first

```
DELETE http://localhost:3030/api/library/<slug>/layout_example
```

---

## Step 1 — Identify all components

List every component before placing anything:
- U1 — the IC
- One bypass/decoupling cap per VCC/VDD/supply pin
- All inductors, resistors shown in the datasheet typical application

---

## Step 2 — Package courtyard dimensions

Use these to compute spacing. No two courtyards may overlap.

| Package | Courtyard half X | Courtyard half Y |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| QFN-12-3x3 | ±2.0 mm | ±2.0 mm |
| QFN-16-3x3 | ±2.45 mm | ±2.45 mm |
| MCLP-4 | ±1.4 mm | ±1.2 mm |
| MCLP-8 | ±1.4 mm | ±1.1 mm |
| MCLP-12 | ±1.6 mm | ±1.4 mm |
| DFN-6-1.8x1.8 | ±1.3 mm | ±1.225 mm |
| DFN-8-2x2 | ±1.4 mm | ±1.325 mm |
| SOT-23 | ±1.6 mm | ±1.1 mm |
| SOT-23-5 | ±1.7 mm | ±1.5 mm |
| SOIC-8 | ±4.0 mm | ±2.8 mm |
| 0201 | ±0.54 mm | ±0.3 mm |
| 0402 | ±1.0 mm | ±0.5 mm |
| 0603 | ±1.5 mm | ±0.6 mm |
| 0805 | ±1.8 mm | ±0.9 mm |

**Minimum center-to-center distance:**
```
min_X = IC_half_X + passive_half_X + 0.25
min_Y = IC_half_Y + passive_half_Y + 0.25
```

Example — QFN-12-3x3 + 0402:
- Horizontal: 2.0 + 1.0 + 0.25 = **3.25 mm minimum**
- Vertical:   2.0 + 0.5 + 0.25 = **2.75 mm minimum**

---

## Step 3 — Placement rules

- Place IC at roughly the board center
- Place each decoupling cap aligned to its supply pin (same X or same Y as the pin)
- Cap connecting pad faces the IC; GND pad faces away
- Board size: 1 mm margin around all component courtyards

---

## Step 4 — Board JSON format

```json
{
  "version": "1.0",
  "title": "<PART_NUMBER> Layout Example",
  "board": { "width": 20, "height": 15, "units": "mm" },
  "components": [
    {
      "id": "U1", "ref": "U1", "value": "<PART_NUMBER>",
      "footprint": "<footprint-name>",
      "x": 10, "y": 7.5, "rotation": 0, "layer": "F",
      "pads": [
        { "number": "1", "name": "VIN", "x": -1.0, "y": 0,
          "type": "smd", "shape": "rect", "size_x": 0.6, "size_y": 1.5, "net": "VIN" }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "nets": [
    { "name": "VIN", "pads": ["U1.1", "C1.1"] }
  ],
  "traces": [
    { "net": "VIN", "segments": [
        { "start": { "x": 9.0, "y": 7.5 }, "end": { "x": 7.5, "y": 7.5 } }
    ]}
  ],
  "vias": []
}
```

Key rules:
- `pads[].x/y` are **relative to component center**
- `traces[].segments[].start/end` are **world coordinates**
- Every signal pad must have a non-empty `net`
- Every net in `nets[]` must list all pad refs (`"REF.padnumber"`)

---

## Step 5 — Save

```
PUT http://localhost:3030/api/library/<slug>/layout_example
Content-Type: application/json

<board JSON>
```

PMA3-83LNW+
PRODUCT OVERVIEW
The PMA3-83LNW+ is a PHEMT based wideband, low noise MMIC amplifier with a unique combination of low noise, high IP3, 
and flat gain over wideband making it ideal for sensitive, high-dynamic-range receiver applications.  This design operates on a 
single 5V or 6V supply, is well matched for 50Ω and comes in a tiny, low profile package (3 x 3 x 0.89mm), accommodating dense 
circuit board layouts.
FeatureAdvantages
Low noise, 1.2 dB at 2 GHzEnables lower system noise figure performance.
High IP3
 y+37 dBm at 2 GHz
 y+29 dBm at 8 GHz
Combination of low noise and high IP3 makes this MMIC amplifier ideal for use in low noise receiver front end 
(RFE) as it gives the user advantages of sensitivity and two-tone IM performance at both ends of the dynamic 
range.
Low operating voltage, 5V/6V.Achieves high IP3 using low voltage.
3 x 3mm 12-lead MCLP package
Tiny footprint saves space in dense layouts while providing low inductance, repeatable transitions, and excellent 
thermal contact to the PCB.
Wide bandwidth with flat gain
 y±0.6 dB over 0.4 to 7 GHz
 y±1.5 dB over 0.4 to 8 GHz
Enables a single amplifier to be used in many wideband applications including defense, instrumentation and more.
